title: Precision gravimetry via harnessing interaction-induced resonances in optical lattices
authors: Hassan Manshouri, Moslem Zarei, Mehdi Abdi, Yasser Omar, Sougato Bose, Abolfazl Bayat
abstract: By confining a Bose-Einstein condensate in a vertical lattice subjected to a gravitational potential, we analyze the quantum Fisher information to determine its scaling with respect to time, system size and particle number. Our results reveal that in the localized phase, on-site interactions $U$ amplify the quantum Fisher information by a factor with respect to resonance condition $U=mh$ where $U$ is factor of gradient field amplitude $h$. This precision enhancement can be employed in gravitational acceleration measurements with a finite number of particles trapped in optical lattices.
summary: The paper shows that by tuning the on-site interactions of a Bose-Einstein Condensate in a vertical optical lattice to match a resonance condition with the gravitational gradient, one can significantly enhance the precision of gravimetry. This resonance-induced amplification allows for better gravitational acceleration measurements using a finite number of particles.
main_problem: Investigating how to enhance the precision of gravitational field (gradient field h) measurements using many-body physics in optical lattices.
main_result: On-site interactions can amplify the Quantum Fisher Information by a factor of approximately 4 when the resonance condition U = mh is met, improving gravitational sensitivity by a factor of approximately 2.
method: Quantum Estimation Theory using the Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) and the Cramér-Rao bound to analyze the scaling of sensing precision.
model_or_system: A Bose-Einstein Condensate (BEC) of ultracold bosonic atoms confined in a vertical 1D optical lattice, modeled by the Stark Bose-Hubbard Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), gravitational sensitivity (delta g/g), site occupation, and the resonance coefficient Ar(U, m).
important_parameters: On-site interaction strength (U), tunneling rate (J), gradient field strength (h), lattice size (L), and particle number (N).
main_assumptions: The system is analyzed in the long-time limit where QFI scales as t^2, and the analysis focuses on the unitary time evolution of the state.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the lattice schematic and particle tunneling at resonance; Fig 2 shows the time evolution of normalized QFI across different phases; Fig 7 plots normalized QFI and the resonance coefficient for different m and N.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of gravimetry, defines the Stark Bose-Hubbard model, analyzes QFI scaling in different phases (localized, transition, extended), quantifies the resonance enhancement via the coefficient Ar, and concludes with the implications for sensitivity.
why_interesting: It demonstrates how many-body interactions, typically seen as a source of complexity, can be precisely tuned to enhance quantum sensing metrology in ultracold atom systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) Schematic of a lattice with the tunneling rate J and gradient field h. (b) Starting with the configuration |...0101010...⟩with the energy E0, the corresponding configurations with the on-site interaction energy at resonance U = mh are drawn where m = 1, 2, 3, 4. colorred (The lattice depth is too small. Enlarge it a bit.)

FIG. 3: In the long time limit, the normalized QFI for N = 2 and different system size L as a function of h/J is plotted at (a) the non-interacting regime (U/J = 0), (b) intermediate interactions (U/J = 5), and (c) the strongly interacting limit (U/J = 20). (d) The normalized QFI as a function of L is plotted. The inset shows the scaling exponent β with respect to gradient field h/J for L=20, in which at the transition point hc/J exhibits β = 2.

FIG. 2: The time evolution of the normalized QFI at different phases for N = 2 particles in an L = 11 lattice. FQ/t2 with respect to time is plotted for different U/J at (a) h/J = 5 in the localized phase, (b) h/J = 1 at the transition point, and (c) h/J = 0.1 in the extended phase. The QFI in the long time limit scales as FQ ∼t2 in all plots.

FIG. 5: (a) FQ is plotted as a function of various on-site interaction energy and gradient field for L = 11 and N = 4. (b) Shows the normalized QFI for different U/J divided by the non-interacting normalized QFI, FQ/t2(U=0). The lines U = mh are signified with the color lines blue (m=1), orange (m=2), green (m=3) and red (m=4).

FIG. 4: Particle-number scaling of the normalized quantum Fisher information FQ/t2 in the long-time limit. FQ/t2 as a function of h/J for L = 11 of different N is figured in (a) non-interacting regime (U = 0), (b) intermediate regime (U = 5), and strongly interacting limit (U/J = 20). (d) The QFI scales as FQ/t2 ∼N α, in which for all phases α = 1, while in the extended phase becomes larger than the localized phase and the transition point value.
